member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Capabilities.TestFramework.IBannerMessageOwnerCapability
member.summary:
An optional test framework capability that allows the test framework to provide the banner message to the platform. If the message is null or if the capability is not present, the platform will use its default banner message.
This capability implementation allows to abstract away the various conditions that the test framework may need to consider to decide whether or not the banner message should be displayed.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Extensions.IBlockingDataConsumer
member.summary: Marker interface for a that must consume the data inline, before the call to publish the data returns to the producer.

remarks.para: A regular consumes data asynchronously: the data is queued and processed on a background loop, so there is no guarantee about when runs relative to the producer continuing its work. A consumer that additionally implements is instead invoked inline by the message bus, so the producer's call to publish does not return until has completed. This is useful for extensions that need to guarantee a piece of work happens before the producer proceeds (for example, before a test starts running).

Because consumption happens inline, any exception thrown by propagates back to the producer that published the data, rather than being observed later during drain. Implementations should therefore avoid throwing for non-fatal conditions.

Inline consumption is serialized: only one call runs at a time per consumer. As a consequence, a blocking consumer must not, from within its , publish data that is routed back to itself, as that would deadlock. Note that publishing data where the producer is the consumer itself (same UID) is safe, because the message bus skips delivering a producer's data back to that same producer; the deadlock risk is specifically re-entrant publishing that routes back to this consumer under a different producer UID.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Extensions.TestHostControllers.ITestHostHandle
member.summary: Represents a launched test host that the platform monitors for completion.
member.remarks: The handle is intentionally agnostic of the underlying launch mechanism: it does not assume a local OS process. is therefore an optional, free-form string used only for diagnostics; the platform tracks completion through , , and . The platform owns the handle for the whole lifetime of the test host and disposes it (via ) once the host has exited, so implementations should release any OS resources they hold (process objects, sockets, container clients, …) in .

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Extensions.TestHostControllers.ITestHostHandle.Identifier
member.summary: Gets an optional, free-form identifier for the launched test host, used only for diagnostics.
member.remarks: The value can be anything meaningful for the launch mechanism — for example a process id, a container id, or a remote host:pid. Returns when the launcher has nothing useful to surface. The platform never relies on this value for control flow.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Extensions.TestHostControllers.ITestHostHandle.ExitCode
member.summary: Gets the exit code of the test host.
member.remarks: Only valid once is (or after has completed). Reading it while the host is still running is undefined; implementations are not required to throw.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Extensions.TestHostControllers.ITestHostHandle.HasExited
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the test host has exited.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Extensions.TestHostControllers.ITestHostHandle.WaitForExitAsync(System.Threading.CancellationToken)
member.summary: Waits asynchronously for the test host to exit, or for to be canceled. The platform may await this more than once.
member.param: A token that cancels the wait.
member.returns: A task that completes when the test host has exited.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Extensions.TestHostControllers.ITestHostHandle.Terminate
member.summary: Terminates the test host. The platform calls this for best-effort teardown (for example when hang dump decides to abort the run).

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Extensions.TestHostControllers.ITestHostLauncher
member.summary: Allows an extension to control how the out-of-process test host is launched, replacing the platform's default Process.Start behavior.
member.remarks: The platform keeps owning everything around the launch — argument and environment preparation, the controller-to-host IPC pipe, the PID handshake, callbacks, and exit-code reconciliation — and delegates only the single "create and start the test host" step to the registered launcher. The launcher does not have to start a local OS process: it can deploy and activate a packaged application, launch a container, or start the host on a remote machine, as long as it returns an the platform can monitor.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Extensions.TestHostControllers.ITestHostLauncher.LaunchTestHostAsync(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Extensions.TestHostControllers.TestHostLaunchContext,System.Threading.CancellationToken)
member.summary: Creates and starts the test host. The platform has already prepared the file name, arguments, and environment variables (including the controller IPC pipe name) carried by . The implementation must return a handle the platform can monitor for completion.
member.returns: A handle the platform monitors for the lifetime of the test host.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Extensions.TestHostControllers.ITestHostProcessInformation.HasExitedGracefully
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the test host app has exited gracefully.
member.remarks: This flag is based on a message that the TestApplication sends at its end. The test host process might still crash or be killed after sending this message, in which case this flag will be true but the process hasn't truly exited gracefully.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.CommandLine.CommandLineParser.Parse(System.String[],Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Helpers.IEnvironment)
member.summary:
Options parser support: * Only - and -- prefix for options https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/standard/commandline/syntax#options * Multiple option arguments https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/standard/commandline/syntax#multiple-arguments * Use '=' or ':' as the delimiter between an option name and its argument. See https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/standard/commandline/syntax#option-argument-delimiters * escape with \ * surrounding with "" * surrounding with ''
Options parser doesn't support * Default argument/commands/verb * Alias https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/standard/commandline/syntax#aliases * Case sensitivity https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/standard/commandline/syntax#case-sensitivity * -- token https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/standard/commandline/syntax#the----token
https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799/utilities/V3_chap02.html#tag_18_02_03 https://learn.microsoft.com/cpp/c-language/parsing-c-command-line-arguments?view=msvc-170 Double-Quotes: we don't support for now * The dollar-sign shall retain its special meaning introducing parameter expansion * The backquote shall retain its special meaning introducing the other form of command substitution * A POSIX convention lets you omit the delimiter when you are specifying a single-character option alias, i.e. myapp -vquiet.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Configurations.AggregatedConfiguration.TryGetCommandLineOptionFromProviders(System.String,System.Boolean@,System.String[]@)
member.summary: Resolves the value of a command-line option across all registered providers at the option granularity (not per-key), so that CLI provider entries fully shadow JSON entries for the same option even when the storage shapes don't overlap (e.g., CLI --list-tests with no args vs. JSON "list-tests": ["json"]).

remarks.para: Providers are walked in registration order; the first one that has any data for wins and its values are returned exclusively. A bare boolean "false" at a winning provider is treated as an explicit disable and short-circuits the search.

Provider contract assumptions for the commandLineOptions:* section (issue #6349):
TryGet returning true with a null value is treated as if the key were absent at this provider — a custom provider that wants to explicitly shadow lower providers must return either the boolean string "False" (disable) or a non-null argument value.
Indexed argument entries are required to be contiguous starting at :0. A gap stops collection — sparse arrays will silently truncate. The JSON provider always emits contiguous indices for arrays.

member.param: Option name with or without leading --.
Set to true if any provider reports the option as set.
Empty for zero-arity / disabled, otherwise the argument list from the winning provider.
member.returns: true if any provider had any data for the option (even an explicit disable); false if no provider mentioned the option.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Configurations.AggregatedConfiguration.GetTestConfigJsonSection(System.String)
member.summary: Returns the immediate (one-level) string entries declared under in the loaded testconfig.json file. Returns an empty list if no JSON configuration source is active or the section is absent.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Configurations.AggregatedConfiguration.EnumerateJsonCommandLineOptions
member.summary: Returns the typed command-line option entries declared in the loaded testconfig.json file. Returns an empty list if no JSON configuration source is active or the section is absent. Throws if the section contains an unsupported shape (see ).

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Configurations.AggregatedConfiguration.NormalizeJsonCommandLineOptionScalars(System.Collections.Generic.IReadOnlyDictionary{System.String,Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Extensions.CommandLine.CommandLineOption})
member.summary:
Normalizes JSON-sourced scalar command-line option entries to the indexed shape for any option in with >= 1, so that subsequent and validator passes see a uniform representation. See
for the full rationale.
member.remarks: A no-op when no JSON configuration source is registered. Safe to call at most once after the option registry is fully assembled and before any consumer reads command-line option values from .

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Configurations.CommandLineConfigurationProvider
member.summary: that exposes the parsed CLI options under the commandLineOptions:* section.

remarks.para: Storage layout (mirrors the layout that JsonConfigurationFileParser produces for the commandLineOptions JSON section, so the same lookup helpers serve both sources):

item.description: Zero-arity option present (e.g. --hangdump): stores commandLineOptions:<name> = "true" as a presence marker.
Single- or multi-value option (e.g. --hangdump-timeout 5m or --filter-uid a b): stores one entry per argument under commandLineOptions:<name>:<index>.

remarks.para: Option names are stored without the leading -- prefix and compared case-insensitively to match semantics.

An option that appears multiple times on the command line is flattened into a single sequence of arguments (matching ), so the resulting key/value layout is identical to what a JSON array under the same option name would produce.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Configurations.CommandLineConfigurationSource
member.summary: that exposes the parsed CLI options through the unified read model (issue #6349).

remarks.para: Registered with 0 so that CLI-provided values always win against values from environment variables (, Order 1) and from testconfig.json (, Order 3). See for the first-hit-wins lookup semantics.
The provider built by this source mirrors the JSON-flattened layout, allowing both the CLI and commandLineOptions in testconfig.json to be merged transparently. See for the key shape.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Configurations.ConfigurationExtensions.IsCommandLineOptionSet(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Configurations.IConfiguration,System.String)
member.summary: Determines whether the given CLI option name is set, consulting all registered instances (CLI, environment variables, JSON, ...) under the commandLineOptions:* section.

remarks.para: Used by so JSON-sourced options are transparently visible to all consumers (issue #6349).

Resolution is performed at the option granularity by walking the configuration providers in registration order via : the first provider that has any data for the option wins outright and its sibling entries are not merged with later providers. This guarantees that, e.g., an explicit zero-arity --list-tests on the CLI is not silently overridden by an indexed JSON array under the same option.

When the instance is not an (test mocks etc.), falls back to a merged-view lookup via the IConfiguration[key] indexer. In that path the providers cannot be walked, so precedence is per-key rather than per-option.

Note: a JSON bare scalar that happens to be the string "true" or "false" for an option that takes arguments is ambiguous — it is interpreted here as a presence/absence marker rather than as the argument value. Authors should use the array form (["true"], ["false"]) when the literal string is intended.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Configurations.ConfigurationExtensions.TryGetCommandLineOptionArguments(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Configurations.IConfiguration,System.String,System.String[]@)
member.summary: Returns the argument list for the given CLI option name. Sees values from every registered (CLI, env vars, JSON, ...).

remarks.para: Used by so JSON-sourced options are transparently visible to all consumers (issue #6349).
Resolution mirrors : providers are walked in registration order and the first one with data for the option wins entirely, so CLI/JSON values for the same option never interleave.

Behavior at the winning provider is intentionally a superset of so existing consumers continue to work:
Walks commandLineOptions:<name>:0, :1, ... until a gap is encountered, returning the collected list when non-empty.
Otherwise consults the bare key commandLineOptions:<name>: null => option absent at this provider; a boolean ("true"/"false", case-insensitive) => presence marker (true => set with zero args; false => explicitly not set); any other string => single-element array.

JSON-sourced entries are subject to arity, unknown-option, and per-argument validation in (the validator is given the same JSON view via ), so a JSON typo or bad value fails at startup with the same diagnostics as on the CLI.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Configurations.JsonConfigurationSource.JsonConfigurationProvider.GetSection(System.String)
member.summary: Returns the immediate (one-level) string entries under from the loaded JSON configuration file.
member.remarks:
The method enforces a strict schema for the section:
The section value must be a JSON object (not a scalar, an array, etc.).
Each direct child must be a scalar (string/number/bool/null) — the parser converts non-strings to text.
Nested objects or arrays are rejected; an explicit empty object/array entry is also rejected.
Throws with a descriptive message (including the configuration file path) when the schema is violated.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Configurations.JsonConfigurationSource.JsonConfigurationProvider.EnumerateCommandLineOptions
member.summary: Enumerates the commandLineOptions section of the loaded testconfig.json file as a list of typed entries, applying the strict schema documented at .

remarks.para:
Supported per-option JSON shapes:
"foo": "bar" — scalar argument (single value).
"foo": true — presence marker, equivalent to --foo on the CLI.
"foo": false — explicit disable; surfaced via .
"foo": ["a", "b"] — multi-value argument list.

Rejected shapes (throw ):
Nested objects like "foo": { "bar": "x" }.
Array of objects like "foo": [ { "bar": "x" } ].
Mixing scalar and indexed entries for the same option name (defensive guard; cannot happen from JSON input but possible if a custom provider mutates the underlying maps).
Empty containers ("foo": []) are skipped (treated as absent). Empty objects ("foo": {}) are rejected because that shape is otherwise indistinguishable from a malformed nested entry.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Configurations.JsonConfigurationSource.JsonConfigurationProvider.NormalizeCommandLineOptionScalars(System.Collections.Generic.IReadOnlyDictionary{System.String,Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Extensions.CommandLine.CommandLineOption})
member.summary: Rewrites scalar commandLineOptions:<name> entries to the indexed shape (commandLineOptions:<name>:0) for options registered with >= 1, using as the option registry.

remarks.para: The CLI-backed configuration provider stores zero-arity flags at the bare option key and arg-bearing options under indexed keys (one per argument). The JSON provider cannot distinguish these shapes at parse time because the user freely writes either "foo": "value" (scalar) or "foo": ["value"] (array). For arg-bearing options that always require at least one argument, a scalar value is always the first argument — never a presence marker. Storing it under the indexed key normalizes the shape so both and see one consistent representation.

In particular, this fixes the JSON scalar/bool ambiguity from #6349/#8830: a value like "my-option": "true" for an arg-bearing option used to be misinterpreted as a presence marker (zero arguments) instead of being passed as the first argument value.

Optional-arg options (Min == 0 && Max >= 1) are left untouched because either interpretation (presence vs. scalar argument) is semantically valid; users who need to disambiguate should use the explicit array form.

member.name: F:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Configurations.PlatformConfigurationConstants.CommandLineOptionsSectionName
member.summary: Root section name used by the unified configuration model to expose command-line options through . Both the in-memory CLI-backed provider () and the JSON-backed provider flatten individual options under commandLineOptions:<name> (single value or boolean presence marker) and commandLineOptions:<name>:<index> (one entry per argument for multi-value options).

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Helpers.ObjectPool`1
member.summary:
Generic implementation of object pooling pattern with predefined pool size limit. The main purpose is that limited number of frequently used objects can be kept in the pool for further recycling.
Notes: 1) it is not the goal to keep all returned objects. Pool is not meant for storage. If there is no space in the pool, extra returned objects will be dropped.
2) it is implied that if object was obtained from a pool, the caller will return it back in a relatively short time. Keeping checked out objects for long durations is ok, but reduces usefulness of pooling. Just new up your own.
Not returning objects to the pool in not detrimental to the pool's work, but is a bad practice. Rationale: If there is no intent for reusing the object, do not use pool - just use "new".

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Helpers.ObjectPool`1.Factory
member.remarks: Not using System.Func{T} because this file is linked into the (debugger) Formatter, which does not have that type (since it compiles against .NET 2.0).

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Helpers.ObjectPool`1.Allocate
member.summary: Produces an instance.
member.remarks: Search strategy is a simple linear probing which is chosen for it cache-friendliness. Note that Free will try to store recycled objects close to the start thus statistically reducing how far we will typically search.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Helpers.ObjectPool`1.Free(`0,System.Threading.CancellationToken)
member.summary: Returns objects to the pool.
member.remarks: Search strategy is a simple linear probing which is chosen for it cache-friendliness. Note that Free will try to store recycled objects close to the start thus statistically reducing how far we will typically search in Allocate.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Helpers.ObjectPool`1.ForgetTrackedObject(`0,`0)
member.summary:
Removes an object from leak tracking.
This is called when an object is returned to the pool. It may also be explicitly called if an object allocated from the pool is intentionally not being returned to the pool. This can be of use with pooled arrays if the consumer wants to return a larger array to the pool than was originally allocated.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.IPC.NamedPipeClient.#ctor(System.String,Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Helpers.IEnvironment,System.Boolean)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class connecting to the named pipe .
member.param: The OS-level named pipe name to connect to.
The environment abstraction used for process-exit on connection loss.

When (the default) a lost connection - the server disconnecting while we write a request or before it sends a response - terminates the process via . This is the right behavior for the primary data pipe: if we cannot talk to the host there is no way to recover. Set it to for auxiliary channels (e.g. the reverse server-control pipe) where a dropped connection should surface as an exception the caller can handle cooperatively rather than killing the whole test host.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Logging.LogLevel
member.summary: An enumeration of the supported logging levels.

member.name: F:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Logging.LogLevel.Trace
member.summary: Logs that contain the most detailed messages. These messages may contain sensitive application data. These messages are disabled by default and should never be enabled in a production environment.

member.name: F:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Logging.LogLevel.Debug
member.summary: Logs that are used for interactive investigation during development. These logs should primarily contain information useful for debugging and have no long-term value.

member.name: F:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Logging.LogLevel.Information
member.summary: Logs that track the general flow of the application. These logs should have long-term value.

member.name: F:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Logging.LogLevel.Warning
member.summary: Logs that highlight an abnormal or unexpected event in the application flow, but do not otherwise cause the application execution to stop.

member.name: F:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Logging.LogLevel.Error
member.summary: Logs that highlight when the current flow of execution is stopped due to a failure. These should indicate a failure in the current activity, not an application-wide failure.

member.name: F:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Logging.LogLevel.Critical
member.summary: Logs that describe an unrecoverable application or system crash, or a catastrophic failure that requires immediate attention.

member.name: F:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Logging.LogLevel.None
member.summary: Not used for writing log messages. Specifies that a logging category should not write any messages.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.AzureDevOpsCommandOutputDeviceData
member.summary: Marks an output-device line produced by the AzureDevOpsReport extension that must reach the Azure DevOps pipeline log even under the dotnet test pipe protocol. This is usually an Azure DevOps logging command (for example ##[group], ##[endgroup] or ##vso[...]), but it also carries the extension's other report output, such as the slow-test progress lines.
member.remarks: In a single-assembly run this renders like any other (the terminal output device writes its verbatim). Under the dotnet test pipe protocol the host installs , which recognizes this marker and forwards the line to the SDK over the protocol (version 1.2.0+), so the AzureDevOpsReport output is not swallowed in multi-assembly runs.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.AzureDevOpsLogIssueFormatter
member.summary: Helpers for translating output-device messages into Azure Pipelines logging commands (##vso[...]) so that errors and warnings surface on the Azure DevOps build / pipeline summary instead of being just colored text in the agent log. See https://github.com/microsoft/testfx/issues/5979.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.AzureDevOpsLogIssueFormatter.IsAzureDevOpsAgent(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Helpers.IEnvironment)
member.summary: Returns true when the current process is running on an Azure DevOps agent (TF_BUILD=true), regardless of the TESTINGPLATFORM_AZDO_OUTPUT opt-out. Use this for the AzureDevOpsReport extension's explicit --report-azdo output (the user opted in via the option), and reserve for the platform's automatic ##vso[task.logissue] emission, which the opt-out disables.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.AzureDevOpsLogIssueFormatter.IsAzureDevOpsEnvironment(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Helpers.IEnvironment)
member.summary: Returns true when the current process is running on an Azure DevOps agent (TF_BUILD=true) and the user has not opted out via TESTINGPLATFORM_AZDO_OUTPUT=off|false|0.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.AzureDevOpsLogIssueFormatter.FormatLogIssue(System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Formats a message as a ##vso[task.logissue type=<severity>]<message> line with the standard Azure Pipelines escaping rules applied to the message body.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.AzureDevOpsLogIssueFormatter.Escape(System.String)
member.summary: Escapes a value for inclusion in the message body of an Azure Pipelines logging command per the official escaping rules (formatting commands): % -> %25, ; -> %3B, \r -> %0D, \n -> %0A, ] -> %5D. % must be escaped first so the other replacements don't get double-encoded.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.DiscoveredTestsJsonSerializer
member.summary: Serializes the result of --list-tests json into a single JSON document with as much information as is available for each .
member.remarks:
Schema history (bump on any breaking change):
Initial schema. Top-level: schemaVersion (int), tests (array of test objects). Per test: uid, displayName, plus optional type (assembly / namespace / type / method / arity / params / return — from ), location (file path, start line, end line — from ), traits (array of { key, value } from ), properties (array of { key, value } from ; array — not object — so duplicate keys survive serialization). Absent fields are omitted; type.namespace is omitted for the global namespace.

member.name: F:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.DiscoveredTestsJsonSerializer.SchemaVersion
member.summary: Schema version of the produced JSON document. Increment when introducing a breaking change.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.DiscoveredTestsJsonSerializer.Serialize(System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable{Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Extensions.Messages.TestNode})
member.summary: Serializes the discovered tests to a pretty-printed JSON document.
member.returns: The JSON document.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.DotnetTestPassthroughOutputDevice
member.summary:
The host output device used under the dotnet test pipe protocol. Like it discards regular (informational) host output (the SDK's TerminalTestReporter owns user-facing rendering), but it additionally forwards two classes of host message to the SDK over the pipe so they are not swallowed in multi-assembly runs:
Lines marked with are forwarded verbatim as so the AzureDevOpsReport extension's logging commands (##[group], ##vso[...]) still reach the pipeline log (protocol 1.2.0+, only on an Azure DevOps agent where the extension produces them).
and are forwarded as so host-side diagnostics produced outside test results (hang/crash dump diagnostics, retry summaries, generic extension/framework warnings and errors) reach the SDK, which routes them to its TerminalTestReporter's WriteWarningMessage / WriteErrorMessage (protocol 1.3.0+).
member.remarks: Forwarding is gated on the SDK negotiating the relevant protocol version ( for Azure DevOps commands, for display messages); against an older SDK the corresponding lines are swallowed exactly like the no-op device, so no unknown message id is ever sent. Informational output (plain / that is not an Azure DevOps marker) is still discarded — only warnings and errors cross the wire. The and the dotnet test execution id are resolved lazily because both become available only after the output device is built (the connection is created and the execution id env var is set during AfterCommonServiceSetupAsync).

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.TargetFrameworkParser.GetShortTargetFrameworkIncludingPlatform(System.Reflection.Assembly)
member.summary: Resolves the short target framework moniker of , including the platform component (e.g. net8.0-windows10.0.18362.0) when the assembly was built for a platform-specific TFM, including non-OS / custom platform identifiers such as Uno's browserwasm.
member.remarks: A plain net8.0 build and a net8.0-windows10.0.18362.0 build carry the exact same (.NETCoreApp,Version=v8.0) and produce the same , so the short TFM alone cannot tell them apart. The only runtime-visible signal is System.Runtime.Versioning.TargetPlatformAttribute, which the SDK emits for any platform-specific TFM (including non-OS / custom platform identifiers such as Uno's browserwasm). Appending it here keeps report file names unique per build so two modules of the same assembly no longer overwrite each other's report.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.TargetFrameworkParser.BuildTargetFrameworkMoniker(System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Combines a short target framework (e.g. net8.0) with an optional platform name (e.g. Windows10.0.18362.0) into a full moniker (e.g. net8.0-windows10.0.18362.0).

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.TargetFrameworkParser.GetTargetPlatformName(System.Reflection.Assembly)
member.summary: Reads the platform name from System.Runtime.Versioning.TargetPlatformAttribute on , or when the assembly targets no specific platform (or only carries an empty/whitespace platform value).

member.summary: Determines whether the file system is case sensitive. Originally copied from https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/blob/73ba11f3015216b39cb866d9fb7d3d25e93489f2/src/libraries/Common/src/System/IO/PathInternal.CaseSensitivity.cs#L41-L59 . Upstream has since replaced the probe with an OS-based check; we intentionally keep the probe implementation here.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.IProgressRenderer
member.summary: Strategy that decides how in-progress information is rendered to the terminal.
member.remarks:
Two implementations exist:
redraws the progress block in place on every refresh tick (used for cursor-capable ANSI terminals).
emits single durable lines only when the run goes silent or a test runs slowly (used for and , e.g. CI / piped / redirected output).
All members are invoked by while it holds the terminal lock, except which may be called from the message pump and MUST be thread-safe.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.IProgressRenderer.TickInterval
member.summary: Gets the desired cadence between invocations. Cursor renderers want a responsive sub-second redraw, whereas the silence-driven heartbeat only needs second-level granularity, so a slower cadence avoids unnecessary scan/sort work on every tick in CI / redirected runs.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.IProgressRenderer.OnStart
member.summary: Called once when progress reporting starts, before the first tick.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.IProgressRenderer.OnTick(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.ITerminal,Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TestProgressState[])
member.summary: Called on every refresh tick of the rendering thread.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.IProgressRenderer.OnWrite(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.ITerminal,Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TestProgressState[],System.Action{Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.ITerminal})
member.summary: Called to write durable output to the terminal, giving the renderer a chance to wrap it (e.g. erase and re-render in-place progress around it).

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.IProgressRenderer.OnTestCompleted
member.summary: Notifies the renderer that a test completed. Used by the heartbeat renderer to reset its silence timer. MUST be thread-safe.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.NonAnsiTerminal
member.summary: Non-ANSI terminal that writes text using the standard Console.Foreground color capabilities to stay compatible with standard Windows command line, and other command lines that are not capable of ANSI, or when output is redirected.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.SilenceDrivenHeartbeatRenderer
member.summary:
Renderer for non-cursor terminals ( and , i.e. CI / piped / file-redirected output). Instead of redrawing a progress block in place, it emits single, durable scrollback lines and only when there is a reason to:
E1 silence heartbeat — one summary line when no test completed for the silence threshold.
E2 slow-test surfacing — one line per test that exceeds the slow-test threshold, with exponential backoff.
A healthy fast suite emits zero heartbeat lines.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.SimpleAnsiTerminal
member.summary: Simple terminal that uses 4-bit ANSI for colors but does not move cursor and does not do other fancy stuff to stay compatible with CI systems. The colors are set on start of every line to properly color multiline strings in CI log viewers that reset color state at each newline (e.g. Azure DevOps).

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter
member.summary: Terminal test reporter that outputs test progress and is capable of writing ANSI or non-ANSI output via the given terminal.

member.name: F:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.NewLineStrings
member.summary: The two directory separator characters to be passed to methods like .

member.name: F:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter._assemblies
member.summary: Per-assembly run state, keyed by the caller-provided execution id. The in-process Microsoft.Testing.Platform host registers a single assembly; the dotnet test orchestrator registers one entry per child test assembly. Progress rendering already supports N workers (slots), so this only generalizes the bookkeeping.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.TotalTests
member.summary: Gets the total number of tests across all registered assemblies.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.#ctor(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Helpers.IConsole,Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporterOptions)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class for orchestrator callers that drive cancellation out-of-band (via ) rather than through a cancellation token.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.#ctor(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Helpers.IConsole,System.Func{System.Boolean},Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporterOptions)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class with custom terminal and manual refresh for testing.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.StartCancelling
member.summary: Let the user know that cancellation was triggered.

member.name: F:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter._erroredAssemblies
member.summary: Assemblies that handshaked but then exited with a non-zero code without reporting any failed test (crash, Environment.FailFast, a hang-dump kill, an option rejected after the handshake, ...). Their process output is printed inline when the assembly completes, but in a large multi-assembly run that inline output is scrolled far above the summary, so we also record it here and re-print it in an end-of-run recap.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.RecordErroredAssembly(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TestProgressState,System.Int32,System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Records an assembly that ended unsuccessfully with no failed test so its process output (exit code + stdout + stderr) can be re-printed at the end of the run. Orchestrator-only: only the multi-process dotnet test orchestrator reaches the exit-code overload of .

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.AppendErroredAssemblyRecap(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.ITerminal)
member.summary: Re-print assemblies that errored during the run (non-zero exit with no failed test) so that — as with the handshake-failure recap — the actionable process output is shown at the end of the run rather than buried in the middle of a large multi-assembly run.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.AppendLongDuration(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.ITerminal,System.TimeSpan,System.Boolean,System.Boolean)
member.summary: Appends a long duration in human readable format such as 1h 23m 500ms.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.AppendAssemblySummary(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TestProgressState,Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.ITerminal)
member.summary: Renders the per-assembly summary line (link + result + compact counts + duration). Used by the dotnet test orchestrator (); the in-process host renders a single run-level summary instead.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.AppendAssemblyTestCounts(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.ITerminal,Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TestProgressState)
member.summary: Renders a compact, per-assembly counts block that mirrors the in-progress indicator. Full-ANSI terminals get "[✓P/xF/↓S]" (with optional "/rR"); simple terminals (NoAnsi / SimpleAnsi / CI) get the ASCII "[+P/xF/?S]" form so logs stay font- and encoding-friendly.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.HasHandshakeFailure
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether any assembly failed to handshake (e.g. a child test host process exited before it could start a session). Used by the dotnet test orchestrator to surface a non-success exit even when no test result was ever reported.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.HandshakeFailureCount
member.summary: Gets the number of assemblies that failed to handshake. Counted toward the run summary's "error" line.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.HandshakeFailure(System.String,System.String,System.Int32,System.String,System.String,System.Boolean)
member.summary: Report that an assembly failed to produce a usable handshake. This is an orchestrator-only path (the in-process host always handshakes); it records the failure so flips and the failure is re-printed in the end-of-run recap, and prints the immediate failure context.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.AppendHandshakeFailureRecap(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.ITerminal)
member.summary: Re-print handshake failures captured during the run so that — even when there is a lot of diagnostic output before the summary — the user sees the actionable failure context (assembly, exit code, stdout, stderr) at the end of the run rather than having to scroll back.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.AssemblyRunCompleted(System.String,System.Int32,System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Orchestrator overload: completes an assembly run with the child process exit code and captured output. When the was never registered (the process exited before a usable handshake), the completion is surfaced as a rather than throwing. On a non-zero exit code the executable summary (exit code + stdout/stderr) is printed.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.TestInProgress(System.String,System.String,System.String,System.String,System.String,System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Orchestrator overload (dotnet test): carries the assembly/target-framework/architecture and per-attempt instance id known to the multi-process orchestrator. The shared in-progress tracking is keyed by execution id, so those extra arguments are accepted for signature parity and the overload delegates to the core method.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporter.TestDiscovered(System.String,System.String,System.String,System.String,System.Nullable{System.Int32})
member.summary: Orchestrator overload (dotnet test): the multi-process orchestrator also knows each discovered test's uid, file path and line number. The shared discovery summary currently lists display names only, so those are accepted for signature parity. When is missing the is used as the listed name; when neither is available the test is still counted (so the discovery total stays correct) but no blank entry is added to the summary.

Orchestrator overload (dotnet test): carries the assembly/target-framework/architecture and the per-attempt instance id that the multi-process orchestrator knows. The instance id drives retry attribution in ; assembly/tfm/arch are accepted for signature parity and the future per-test assembly link.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporterOptions.ShowPassedTests
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether we should show passed tests.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporterOptions.MinimumExpectedTests
member.summary: Gets minimum amount of tests to run.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporterOptions.ZeroTestsPolicy
member.summary: Gets the policy that controls whether an all-skipped run is treated as a "zero tests ran" failure.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporterOptions.ShowProgress
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether we should write the progress periodically to screen. When ANSI is allowed we update the progress as often as we can. When ANSI is not allowed we never have progress. This is a callback to nullable bool, because we don't know if we are running as test host controller until after we setup the console. So we should be polling for the value, until we get non-null boolean and then cache that value.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporterOptions.ShowActiveTests
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the active tests should be visible when the progress is shown.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporterOptions.ShowAssembly
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether per-assembly summary lines (with the compact pass/fail/skip counts) should be rendered. Used by the dotnet test orchestrator which runs multiple assemblies; the in-process host leaves this off.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporterOptions.ShowAssemblyStartAndComplete
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether, when is set, the per-assembly summary line is printed mid-stream as each assembly completes.

member.summary: Gets the silence threshold for the non-cursor heartbeat renderer (E1). When no test completes for this duration, a single heartbeat line is emitted. disables the silence heartbeat.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TerminalTestReporterOptions.SlowTestThreshold
member.summary: Gets the per-test threshold for the non-cursor heartbeat renderer (E2). When a single test exceeds this duration, a single "[slow]" line is emitted (with exponential backoff). disables slow-test surfacing.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TestNodeResultsState.GetSingleActiveOrSummaryTask
member.summary:
Returns the single active task to display on a one-line progress row, without allocating a list. This preserves the prior GetRunningTasks(1).FirstOrDefault() semantics:
when there are no running tasks.
The single running task when there is exactly one.
A reusable summary detail with text like "N tests running" when there are multiple.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.Terminal.TestNodeResultsState.GetRunningTasks(System.Int32)
member.summary: Returns a snapshot of currently running tasks, sorted by elapsed time descending and truncated to entries (the last entry becomes a "... N more running" summary detail when truncation occurs).
member.remarks: The returned is a cached buffer reused across calls on the same instance to avoid per-render-tick allocation. Callers MUST NOT call on the same instance again before finishing use of the previously-returned buffer — the next call on that instance will and rebuild it in place, silently invalidating the prior caller's view. Buffers from different instances are independent and may be held concurrently. This type is not designed for concurrent calls on the same instance; the production caller (AnsiTerminalTestProgressFrame) only invokes this from the single-threaded render loop.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.TestRunSummaryHelper
member.summary: Shared helper for computing test run verdicts and formatting plain-text summaries. Used by both (verdict logic) and (full plain-text summary).

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.TestRunSummaryHelper.IsRunFailed(System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Boolean,System.Int32,Microsoft.Testing.Platform.CommandLine.ZeroTestsPolicy)
member.summary: Determines whether the test run should be considered failed based on the given outcome counters and state.
member.remarks: controls whether an all-skipped run is treated as a failed "zero tests" run. Under (the default) only a run that discovered no test at all fails as zero tests; under skipped tests don't count as run, so an all-skipped run also fails. A run where no test was found is always treated as failed regardless of policy.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.TestRunSummaryHelper.GetVerdictText(System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Boolean,System.Int32,System.Boolean,System.Boolean,Microsoft.Testing.Platform.CommandLine.ZeroTestsPolicy)
member.summary: Computes the verdict string for the test run.
member.remarks:
The two orchestrator-only flags surface non-test failures that must not be masked by the test-count wording:
: at least one assembly failed to hand-shake. This escalates to "Failed!" ahead of the "Zero tests ran" branch (a handshake failure is not a benign empty run).
: at least one assembly process ended unsuccessfully (e.g. crashed or returned a non-zero exit code) even though its tests passed. This escalates the verdict wording to "Failed!" but only AFTER the "Zero tests ran" branch, so a project that legitimately contains zero tests (which exits non-zero by design) keeps the "Zero tests ran" wording rather than "Failed!". Note this only affects the displayed verdict text: still treats a zero-test run as a failed run.
In-process callers never have either condition and pass , so their verdict is unchanged. mirrors : under (the default) an all-skipped run is reported as "Passed!" instead of "Zero tests ran". Under an all-skipped run is reported as "Zero tests ran".

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.OutputDevice.TestRunSummaryHelper.FormatSummaryText(System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Int32,System.Boolean,System.Int32,Microsoft.Testing.Platform.CommandLine.ZeroTestsPolicy)
member.summary: Formats a plain-text test run summary with verdict and counts, suitable for console output (no ANSI escape codes). Unlike , this does not include artifacts, duration, or assembly/TFM/architecture context.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Requests.TreeNodeFilter
member.summary: A tree based filter for test execution.

member.name: F:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Requests.TreeNodeFilter.PathSeparator
member.summary: The path separator character.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Requests.TreeNodeFilter.Filter
member.summary: Gets the filter string.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Requests.TreeNodeFilter.ContainsPropertyFilters
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether any filter segment contains a property expression (e.g., Method[Trait=Foo]). When , the argument to is never inspected, and callers may safely pass an empty bag to avoid per-node allocation.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Requests.TreeNodeFilter.MatchesFilter(System.String,Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Extensions.Messages.PropertyBag)
member.summary: Checks whether a node path matches the tree node filter.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Requests.TreeNodeFilter.ParseFilter(System.String)
member.remarks:
The current grammar for the filter looks as follows:
TREE_NODE_FILTER = EXPR ( '/' EXPR )* EXPR = '(' EXPR ')' | EXPR OP EXPR | NODE_VALUE FILTER_EXPR = '(' FILTER_EXPR ')' | TOKEN '=' TOKEN | TOKEN '!=' TOKEN | FILTER_EXPR OP FILTER_EXPR | TOKEN OP = '&' | '|' NODE_VALUE = TOKEN | TOKEN '[' FILTER_EXPR ']' TOKEN = string
OR expressions are supported for a single path segment, for example: /A/B/C/(Test1|Test2).
OR expressions over full paths are not supported, for example: (/A/B/C/Test1)|(/A/B/C/Test2).
member.exception: Exception thrown, if the filter is malformed, for example A(|B) or A).

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.DotnetTestConnection.StartServerControlChannelAsync(System.Func{System.Threading.CancellationToken,System.Threading.Tasks.Task})
member.summary: Opens the reverse "server control" pipe the SDK advertised during the handshake and parks a long-poll on it. The SDK completes that request with a whenever it wants to signal the test host (today only ). On cancel - or when the control pipe drops, which means the host went away - is invoked exactly once so the caller can stop the run cooperatively (preferring a graceful stop so trx/artifacts are still produced).
member.param: The reaction to a server-initiated cancel. It receives the test application cancellation token.
member.remarks: Both the connect and the long-poll run entirely on a background task, so test start is never blocked on this auxiliary channel. This is best-effort: if the control pipe cannot be established the test run continues unaffected (the feature simply stays off). Callers should only invoke this after a successful handshake and when is . It is invoked once per connection on the awaited run path (before the test run starts), so the control-channel fields below are written here and only read later during exit/dispose - the single-threaded lifecycle makes the plain fields safe without extra synchronization.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.ServerMode.IMessageHandler.ReadAsync(System.Threading.CancellationToken)
member.summary: Reads the next payload from the client.
member.remarks: The reading is likely backed by a data stream and the message handler is responsible for reading the data stream and deserialization of the message. If the reading from the stream is canceled will throw an exception. If deserialization of the message fails, also will throw an exception.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.ServerMode.IMessageHandler.WriteRequestAsync(Microsoft.Testing.Platform.ServerMode.RpcMessage,System.Threading.CancellationToken)
member.summary: Writes the next to the client.
member.remarks: The writing is likely backed by a data stream and the message handler is responsible for writing to the data stream and serialization of the message. If serialization of the message fails, will throw an exception.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.ServerMode.RpcMessageFormatting
member.summary: Helpers to render JSON-RPC message members that hold collections or dictionaries. The compiler-generated record would otherwise print these members using the default (e.g. System.Collections.Generic.List`1[...]), which is unreadable when the messages are dumped to the diagnostic logs.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.ServerMode.SerializerUtilities.#cctor
member.summary: Initializes static members of the class. Is a known fact that this serializer jsonite based suffer of boxing/unboxing issue but it's only for netstandard2.0 and we don't optimize for it for now. We aim to rewrite all the serialization with the best perfomance using .NET System.Text.Json api inside the Json.cs file.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Services.IShutdownProgressReporter
member.summary: Tracks units of work that the platform is awaiting during shutdown so that, after cancellation has been requested, the user can be told which extensions are still running and how long each one has been blocking.
member.remarks: Implementations must be thread-safe. Callers wrap shutdown-relevant awaits with using (reporter.Track(uid, displayName, phase)) { await ... }; on the happy path (no cancellation) this is effectively a no-op beyond bookkeeping.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Services.IShutdownProgressReporter.Track(System.String,System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Registers a unit of in-flight work and returns a disposable that removes it.
member.param: Stable identifier of the extension / consumer being awaited.
Human-readable name surfaced to the user.
Short label describing what we are awaiting (e.g. OnTestSessionFinishingAsync).
member.returns: A disposable that removes the tracker when the awaited work completes.

member.summary: The OpenTelemetry provider interface.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Telemetry.IPlatformOpenTelemetryService
member.summary: Defines platform-specific services for OpenTelemetry instrumentation, including activity management and metric creation.
member.remarks: This interface provides abstractions for starting and managing activities, as well as creating counters and histograms for telemetry data collection. It is intended for internal use ONLY as the platform should be dependency free and netstandard2.0 doesn't have the instrumentation APIs.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Testing.Platform.Telemetry.IHistogram`1
member.summary: Defines the contract for a histogram that collects and analyzes values of a specified value type.
member.remarks: Implementations of this interface typically provide methods for recording values, retrieving statistical summaries, and analyzing distributions. Histograms are commonly used for tracking metrics such as latencies, frequencies, or other numeric measurements in performance monitoring and data analysis scenarios.
member.typeparam: The value type of the data points to be recorded in the histogram. Must be a struct.

member.summary: Built-in that reads the environmentVariables section from the loaded testconfig.json file and applies the entries to the test host child process.
member.remarks: When the section is present and non-empty, this provider opts in to the test host controller process model: the current process becomes the controller and spawns the actual test host as a child process with the configured environment variables set on ProcessStartInfo.

member.name: T:System.Runtime.Versioning.SupportedOSPlatformAttribute
member.summary: Records the operating system (and minimum version) that supports an API. Multiple attributes can be applied to indicate support on multiple operating systems.
member.remarks:
Callers can apply a or use guards to prevent calls to APIs on unsupported operating systems.
A given platform should only be specified once.

member.name: T:System.Runtime.Versioning.UnsupportedOSPlatformAttribute
member.summary: Marks APIs that were removed in a given operating system version.
member.remarks: Primarily used by OS bindings to indicate APIs that are only available in earlier versions.

member.summary: Marks APIs that were obsoleted in a given operating system version.
member.remarks: Primarily used by OS bindings to indicate APIs that should not be used anymore.

The message that explains the obsolescence.

member.summary: Gets the message that explains the obsolescence.

member.name: P:System.Runtime.Versioning.ObsoletedOSPlatformAttribute.Url
member.summary: Gets or sets the URL that provides more information about the obsolescence.

member.name: T:System.Runtime.Versioning.SupportedOSPlatformGuardAttribute
member.summary: Annotates a custom guard field, property or method with a supported platform name and optional version. Multiple attributes can be applied to indicate guard for multiple supported platforms.
member.remarks:
Callers can apply a to a field, property or method and use that field, property or method in a conditional or assert statements in order to safely call platform specific APIs.
The type of the field or property should be boolean, the method return type should be boolean in order to be used as platform guard.

member.name: T:System.Runtime.Versioning.UnsupportedOSPlatformGuardAttribute
member.summary: Annotates the custom guard field, property or method with an unsupported platform name and optional version. Multiple attributes can be applied to indicate guard for multiple unsupported platforms.
member.remarks:
Callers can apply a to a field, property or method and use that field, property or method in a conditional or assert statements as a guard to safely call APIs unsupported on those platforms.
The type of the field or property should be boolean, the method return type should be boolean in order to be used as platform guard.

member.name: T:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.DynamicallyAccessedMembersAttribute
member.summary: Indicates that certain members on a specified are accessed dynamically, for example through .
member.remarks:
This allows tools to understand which members are being accessed during the execution of a program.
This attribute is valid on members whose type is or .
When this attribute is applied to a location of type , the assumption is that the string represents a fully qualified type name.
When this attribute is applied to a class, interface, or struct, the members specified can be accessed dynamically on instances returned from calling on instances of that class, interface, or struct.
If the attribute is applied to a method it's treated as a special case and it implies the attribute should be applied to the "this" parameter of the method. As such the attribute should only be used on instance methods of types assignable to System.Type (or string, but no methods will use it there).

member.name: T:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute
member.summary: Suppresses reporting of a specific rule violation, allowing multiple suppressions on a single code artifact.
member.remarks: is different than in that it doesn't have a . So it is always preserved in the compiled assembly.

member.name: M:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute.#ctor(System.String,System.String)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class, specifying the category of the tool and the identifier for an analysis rule.
member.param: The identifier of the analysis rule the attribute applies to.

member.name: P:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute.Category
member.summary: Gets the category identifying the classification of the attribute.
member.remarks: The property describes the tool or tool analysis category for which a message suppression attribute applies.

member.name: P:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute.CheckId
member.summary: Gets the identifier of the analysis tool rule to be suppressed.
member.remarks: Concatenated together, the and properties form a unique check identifier.

member.name: P:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute.Scope
member.summary: Gets or sets the scope of the code that is relevant for the attribute.
member.remarks: The Scope property is an optional argument that specifies the metadata scope for which the attribute is relevant.

member.name: P:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute.Target
member.summary: Gets or sets a fully qualified path that represents the target of the attribute.
member.remarks: The property is an optional argument identifying the analysis target of the attribute. An example value is "System.IO.Stream.ctor():System.Void". Because it is fully qualified, it can be long, particularly for targets such as parameters. The analysis tool user interface should be capable of automatically formatting the parameter.

member.name: P:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute.MessageId
member.summary: Gets or sets an optional argument expanding on exclusion criteria.
member.remarks: The property is an optional argument that specifies additional exclusion where the literal metadata target is not sufficiently precise. For example, the cannot be applied within a method, and it may be desirable to suppress a violation against a statement in the method that will give a rule violation, but not against all statements in the method.

member.name: P:System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.UnconditionalSuppressMessageAttribute.Justification
member.summary: Gets or sets the justification for suppressing the code analysis message.

member.name: T:System.Index
member.summary: Represent a type can be used to index a collection either from the start or the end.

member.name: M:System.Index.#ctor(System.Int32,System.Boolean)
member.summary: Construct an Index using a value and indicating if the index is from the start or from the end.
member.param: The index value. it has to be zero or positive number.
Indicating if the index is from the start or from the end.
member.remarks: If the Index constructed from the end, index value 1 means pointing at the last element and index value 0 means pointing at beyond last element.

member.name: P:System.Index.Start
member.summary: Create an Index pointing at first element.

member.name: P:System.Index.End
member.summary: Create an Index pointing at beyond last element.

member.name: M:System.Index.FromStart(System.Int32)
member.summary: Create an Index from the start at the position indicated by the value.
member.param: The index value from the start.

member.name: M:System.Index.FromEnd(System.Int32)
member.summary: Create an Index from the end at the position indicated by the value.
member.param: The index value from the end.

member.name: P:System.Index.Value
member.summary: Returns the index value.

member.name: P:System.Index.IsFromEnd
member.summary: Indicates whether the index is from the start or the end.

member.name: M:System.Index.GetOffset(System.Int32)
member.summary: Calculate the offset from the start using the giving collection length.
member.param: The length of the collection that the Index will be used with. length has to be a positive value
member.remarks: For performance reason, we don't validate the input length parameter and the returned offset value against negative values. we don't validate either the returned offset is greater than the input length. It is expected Index will be used with collections which always have non negative length/count. If the returned offset is negative and then used to index a collection will get out of range exception which will be same affect as the validation.

member.name: M:System.Range.GetOffsetAndLength(System.Int32)
member.summary: Calculate the start offset and length of range object using a collection length.

member.remarks: For performance reason, we don't validate the input length parameter against negative values. It is expected Range will be used with collections which always have non negative length/count. We validate the range is inside the length scope though.
